title: Network analysis for steady-state current fluctuations under finite affinity: Application to Brownian computation
abstract: A graph-theoretic analysis of the steady-state current noise in master equations under a finite thermodynamic force (affinity) is presented. The incidence matrix twisted by a finite affinity is not orthogonal to the standard cycle space, motivating the introduction of twisted circuit matrices to restore the orthogonality. The resulting twisted-cycle matrix yields an interference-like effect, enabling us to express the signal-to-noise ratio as a quadratic optimization problem in terms of twisted-cycle currents. We apply this framework to a Brownian computation model on a tree-like state-transition diagram with exponential backward branching, finite affinity at each step, and a single reset cycle. In the limit of an infinitely long intended computation path $\ell$, the Fano factor of the reset current undergoes a transition from noiseless to Poissonian behavior at an affinity equal to the logarithm of the number of immediate predecessors $Œ±$. This corresponds to an easy-hard transition in the computational time complexity [K. Okajima, K. Hukushima, arXiv:2512.24728 ], which is not captured by the thermodynamic uncertainty relation. This transition point precisely characterizes the thermodynamic costs of logically irreversible computation: in the absence of affinity, the reset cost scales as $\ln \ell$, whereas reaching the transition point requires a thermodynamic force of order $\ln Œ±$ per step to counteract backward branching.
summary: This paper presents a new graph-theoretic method to calculate current fluctuations in networks under finite thermodynamic force by using 'twisted' matrices to maintain mathematical orthogonality. It applies this to Brownian computation, demonstrating that the thermodynamic cost of computation is linked to a transition between noiseless and Poissonian noise regimes.
main_problem: Developing a graph-theoretic framework to analyze steady-state current fluctuations in Markov master equations under finite thermodynamic force (affinity), where standard orthogonality between cycle and cut spaces is lost.
main_result: The introduction of twisted circuit matrices restores orthogonality and reveals an interference-like effect in the signal-to-noise ratio; specifically, in Brownian computation, a transition from noiseless to Poissonian Fano factor occurs at an affinity equal to the logarithm of the branching factor.
method: Graph-theoretic analysis using twisted incidence, cycle, and cutset matrices, combined with a duality transformation to express the signal-to-noise ratio as a quadratic optimization problem.
model_or_system: A Brownian computation model represented as a directed, tree-like state-transition diagram with exponential backward branching, a single reset cycle, and finite thermodynamic affinity at each transition step.
key_observables: Steady-state edge currents, Fano factor of the reset current, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and entropy production rates.
important_parameters: Thermodynamic affinity (or bias beta), branching factor (alpha), and the length of the computation path (l).
main_assumptions: Assumes a connected graph with a unique steady state, a directed rooted spanning tree, and focuses on the long-measurement-time limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates fundamental cutset vectors and twisted matrix configurations; Figure 3 shows the state-transition diagram of the Brownian computation model; Figure 4 shows the dependence of the average reset current on parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a new twisted matrix formalism for non-equilibrium networks, applies this framework to a specific Brownian computation model, analyzes the resulting phase transitions in current fluctuations and complexity, and compares results against thermodynamic uncertainty relations.
why_interesting: The paper provides a rigorous topological method for calculating fluctuations in non-equilibrium steady states, which is directly applicable to the study of dissipation and noise in open quantum systems and driven many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
